title: Configuring Uniface on iSeries
breadcrumbs: Installing and Configuring Uniface on iSeries
text: Configuring Uniface on iSeries To configure Uniface, you need to edit assignment files of the Uniface Router, Uniface Servers, and client applications so that they can locate and access databases and files on iSeries. During installation, the installer creates the following assignment file members: DBMS—assignment file member that specifies the DBMS connector and location. UROUTER—assignment file member for the Uniface Router, containing server name (UST) declarations that specify Uniface Servers with different parameters. USYS—global client assignment file member, containing settings that are used by all client applications for objects that are not assigned locally. WASV—client assignment file member for the Uniface Web Application Server UDEPLOY—client assignment file member for the Uniface Deployment Utility After installing Uniface, perform the following configuration steps: Ensure that a collection exists into which data can be stored, or from which data can be retrieved. A collection is a library that contains DB2 catalog tables, a journal named QSQJRN, and one or more journal receivers named QSQJRN xxxx , where xxxx is a number starting at 0001. SQL is used to create tables in a collection that will hold your business data. The collection to be used can be an existing collection in which data resides. Otherwise, create a new collection. To do this, use the STRSQL CL command and issue this SQL statement: create collection LibraryName Copy Obtain the name of the local relational database directory entry to be used by Uniface. To find out the name, enter the command WRKRDBDIRE . This shows all relational databases known to the system. The one that has *LOCAL as its Remote Location is the one you need. For example, if there is a line that looks like the following (the text at the end may be different): _ MY_AS400 *LOCAL Entry added by system Copy then MY_AS400 is the name you need. If there is no local database entry, create one. Use WRKRDBDIRE , enter a 1 on the top line in the Option column and a name for your local relational database. Then press Enter . On the next screen fill in *LOCAL in the Remote Location field and *IP in the Type field and press Enter . The name you have chosen will be the name of the local relational database entry to use in the following step. Modify the member DBMS to specify the correct DBMS connector and location. The member DBMS contains the following settings (assuming the DB2 U4.0 connector): [DRIVER_SETTINGS] DB2 U4.0 USYS$DB2_PARAMS segmented fields : off [PATHS] ; In the DB2 path below, replace RDBDIRE by the ; name of the local relational database. ; Use WRKRDBDIRE to obtain this name. ; Also replace LIBRARY with the name of the ; collection that the userver should use by default. ; Specify a username and password if you need the ; DB2 U4.0 connector to run in Server mode, otherwise ; it runs in Normal mode. ; See the DB2 Connector documentation ; for more information about Server mode. $DB2 = DB2:RDBDIRE:LIBRARY|| $SYS $DB2 $UUU $SYS $IDF $SYS $DEF $SYS Copy In the $DB2 assignment, replace the RDBDIRE and LIBRARY with the relational database directory entry name and the collection name. Note: Be careful not to delete the first colon after DB2 , the colon after the end of the database directory name, or the vertical bars. For more information, see Configuring DB2 Database Access on iSeries . Modify the UROUTER assignment file member to specify USTs that can be used to start the Uniface Server with different parameters. After installation it will only contain the USTs default and wasv . You will probably want to add your own. For more information, see Configuring the Uniface Router on iSeries . Modify your client assignment file to define a path to the Uniface Router on iSeries. In the [PATHS] section, specify a TCP path to the Uniface Server, using the following syntax: $DB2 TCP: iSeriesMachineName + PortNumber | User | Password { | UST } { /ex } iSeriesMachineName —network node name for the host, for example my_as400 or 127.0.0.1 The format of iSeriesMachineName determines the TCP protocol version to be used. PortNumber —port number on which the Uniface Router listens; the default is 13001 . For more information, see Ensure TCP/IP Connectivity . User and Password —depending on system value QPWDLVL , user names and passwords can be case-sensitive. UST —Uniface Server Type; specifies the name of a specific Uniface Server that is declared in the [SERVERS] section of the Uniface Router assignment file, for example DATA or wasv . If UST is omitted, the default server type is used, which is default . /ex —Uniface Server running on iSeriesMachineName is exclusive for this client session. The Uniface Server will not be shared with other client sessions. For more information, see /ex . Example: Uniface Server Connector Paths For example: The following assignment causes the Uniface Router to start a shared Uniface Server of type ANY on your iSeries: $ANY TCP:iSeries+13001|user|pass Copy This server can act as a database server, application server, or file server. Because it is shared, multiple clients (of the same user, and with the same UST) can sometimes share the same Uniface Server. The following assignment causes the Uniface Router to start a shared Uniface Server of type DB2 (a database server) on your iSeries: $DB2 TCP:iSeries+13001|user|pass + DB2: RDBDIRE : LIBRARY || Copy The RDBDIRE and LIBRARY in this line are the same as in the DBMS’s assignment file on the server—they must be replaced by actual values as described above. The following assignment causes the Uniface Router to start a shared Uniface Server using the parameters specified by the wasv UST, defined in the Uniface Router assignment file: $WASV = TCP:iSeries+13001|username|password|wasv Copy The following assignment causes the Uniface Router to start an exclusive Uniface Server of type ANY: $DB2 = TCP:iSeries+13001|user|pass -ex Copy Related Links Paths and Path Assignments A path is a named route to a resource required by the application, such as a database connector and database, a remote server, or a file location. Path-to-Connector Assignments A path-to-connector assignment describes how to access a specific resource via the appropriate network or database connector. It defines the path name, and assigns it to a configuration that specifies the connector to use, the name and location of the resource, and the credentials required to log on to it. Paths are defined in the [PATHS] section of the application assignment file. Configuring the Uniface Server You need to configure the Uniface Server so that it can find the databases and Uniface files, entities, and components that it needs. Configuring Uniface and Uniface Applications Deploying a Uniface enterprise application is largely a matter of configuring the environment to ensure that all components are running in the appropriate locations and that Uniface is able to find them. Configuration files ensure that the Uniface runtime environment can locate and initialize the application components correctly. Configuring the Uniface Router on iSeries After installation, the Uniface Router runs out of the box, either by starting the subsystem or, if you elected not to create a subsystem, by calling the STARTUP program. However, in a typical Uniface environment there are multiple UST's configured to handle different types of requests. These user-specific UST's must be manually configured after installation.
